summary: Patrick McKenzie announces moving his blog from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation at kalzumeus.com. He explains the origin of the Kalzumeus name — a dragon character from a story he wrote years ago — and acknowledges it's not ideal branding. The bulk of the post provides a detailed technical walkthrough for migrating a WordPress.com blog to a self-hosted domain without losing search engine rankings or breaking existing links. He uses the 301 redirect approach via WordPress's $10/year domain mapping feature as a bridge to preserve SEO juice during the transition.
key_points: Self-hosting gives control over domain, design, commercialization, and integration with other content on the same domain
The key to migrating from WordPress.com without losing SEO value is using their $10/year domain mapping as an intermediate step that provides 301 redirects
Bad naming choices early on stick with you — obscure in-jokes that are hard to spell make poor brands
Side projects often die not from bad ideas but from day job demands and lost faith in the business model
money_quotes: If you're just starting out your uISV journey, do as I say and not as I do here: obscure in-jokes which are not easy to spell are probably not the best of ideas.
I swear, even in the all-Java, all-the-time world of my day job I find myself dreaming in Rails.
He was a sarcastic, intelligent, and slightly hyperactive dragon, and makes as good a mascot as any.
You're now paying WordPress $10 a year for, essentially, a 301 redirect from your old blog to your new blog.
Nobody will even notice the second changeover, including Google.
key_insight: When migrating a blog to a new domain, preserving your search engine equity through proper 301 redirects is more important than the move itself.
